title: The jaundiced child whose red cells run out of ATP
blurb: A chronic non-spherocytic hemolytic anemia from the second-commonest glycolytic enzyme defect — the red cell simply can't make enough ATP.

patient.complaint: Long-standing pallor and jaundice, gallstones found on ultrasound.

opening: 4-year-old boy of Northern European descent with lifelong pallor, intermittent scleral icterus, and splenomegaly. An ultrasound for vague abdominal pain shows pigment gallstones. Hgb 8.2, reticulocytes 9%, indirect bilirubin high, haptoglobin low; the smear shows echinocytes ('burr cells') but NO spherocytes and a negative Coombs. What do you want to ask, examine, or order next?
history: Neonatal jaundice requiring phototherapy. Chronic anemia since infancy, transfused twice during viral illnesses. Parents are unaffected (autosomal recessive). No drug exposures. Splenomegaly noted for years.
exam: Pallor, mild scleral icterus, spleen tip 4 cm below costal margin. Frontal bossing (chronic marrow expansion). No lymphadenopathy. Growth at 25th centile.

prompts.mechanism: Briefly: why are RED CELLS uniquely affected, and why is exercise tolerance paradoxically preserved by the high 2,3-BPG?

pinpoint.prompt: Click the glycolytic step that fails in this hemolytic anemia.
pinpoint.correctMsg: Yes — pyruvate kinase. It catalyzes the second ATP-generating (substrate-level) step. Red cells have no mitochondria, so glycolysis is their ONLY ATP source; without PK they can't maintain membrane ion pumps and lyse. Upstream intermediates back up, raising 2,3-BPG (which right-shifts O₂ delivery, partly offsetting the anemia).
pinpoint.wrongMsg: Not quite. Look for the final, ATP-generating step of glycolysis (PEP → pyruvate).
pinpoint.revealMsg: Target: PK (PEP → Pyruvate). RBCs depend entirely on glycolytic ATP; PK is the second of the two ATP-yielding steps.

mcqs.stem: Why does PK deficiency right-shift the oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation curve, easing symptoms?
choices: Upstream glycolytic intermediates accumulate, raising 2,3-BPG, which lowers Hb O₂ affinity and improves tissue delivery
mcqs.explanation: The block at PK backs up 1,3-BPG / 3-PG, feeding 2,3-BPG production. High 2,3-BPG right-shifts the curve, so a given hemoglobin delivers more O₂ — patients tolerate anemia better than the Hb number suggests.

mcqs.stem: Why are mature red cells, but not most other tissues, crippled by this defect?
choices: RBCs lack mitochondria and depend solely on glycolysis for ATP
RBCs lack pyruvate kinase only in disease
RBCs cannot use the pentose phosphate pathway
mcqs.explanation: Anucleate mature RBCs have no mitochondria and no oxidative phosphorylation — glycolysis is their entire ATP economy. Lose a glycolytic ATP step and the Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase fails, the membrane stiffens, and the spleen culls the cells.

mcqs.stem: What is definitive management for severe transfusion-dependent PK deficiency?
choices: Splenectomy reduces hemolysis; the PK activator mitapivat can raise hemoglobin
mcqs.explanation: Splenectomy lessens extravascular hemolysis (the spleen removes the rigid cells). The allosteric PK activator mitapivat is a newer disease-specific option. Watch for iron overload from chronic transfusion.
